stories.title: The Scar-Merchant's Ledger
stories.text:
Korvin the Copper-Handed had dealt in shadow-fragments for three seasons before the market swallowed him whole. The Wailing Scar—that impossible market existing in three places at once—had taught him that the most dangerous commodity wasn't memory or stolen identity. It was regret. He stood in the Spice Quarter of Vethrim, watching refugees trade copper coins for bread that wouldn't fill their bellies. The Remaking had made gold into dust, but it had made conscience into currency, and Korvin's conscience was bankrupt. A woman approached—scarred face, one eye white with cataract. "You brokered the sale of my daughter's laugh," she said. Not a question. Korvin's fingers—all copper from wrist to nail—trembled. He'd worn the name "Copper-Handed" like armor. Now it felt like a brand. "I can buy it back," he whispered. "You can't buy back what's already been remade into someone else's joy," the woman said. "But you can stop selling pieces of people to feed your own hunger."

She pressed something into his palm: a ledger written in blood-ink, its pages endless. Every transaction.

stories.title: The Griffin-Keeper's Hunt
stories.text:
Ashara had trained her griffin Valorix in solitude for thirty winters, but the bird's restlessness announced what Ashara refused to admit: Valorix was dying. The golden feathers that had caught light like liquid fire were dulling, fading to grey at the edges. The griffin's screams—once thunderous enough to shatter stone—had become thin, almost mournful. In the High Meadows of the Steppe Marches, where wind carried voices from older worlds, Isara the Falconer found them hunting. "Your bird is unmated," Isara said, perched on a wind-scoured outcrop with her own hawk—ordinary by comparison, but brilliant in its steadiness. "That's the wasting. Griffins are solitary only if they're broken first."

Ashara's jaw tightened. She'd never asked Valorix's history before the fortress. "There's a glacier three days north," Isara continued. "Where the old griffins nest. Your Valorix remembers that place. I can smell it in her musk."

The journey took a week. Valorix flew slower, resting often. But as they climbed toward the ice-fields, the griffin's eyes cleared. A sound—ancient, trembling, achingly alive—answered from the peaks.

stories.title: The Bone-Thief's Price
stories.text:
Corvhen the Bone-Thief had stolen seventeen reliquaries from the Catacombs Beneath Anthrim—each one carved with Elder Tongue syllables, each one cradling a consciousness-fragment worth more than kingdoms. The Dominion wanted them burned. The bone-singers wanted them freed. Corvhen wanted them to stay hidden. But theft has echoes, and those echoes were catching up to her in the night-markets of Keshtir. The woman who cornered her in the Lampwick Quarter bore the marks of a bone-singer: ink-dark fingers, a voice that hummed at frequencies normal speech couldn't reach. "You took my grandmother's fragments," the woman said. Her name was Naela. "Three pieces of her mind, locked away where she can't hear the Elder songs."

Corvhen drew a blade—not iron, but something older, something carved from starfall bone. "Your grandmother's fragments were made into weapons by the Dominion," Corvhen spat. "I was *liberating* them."

"You were burying them," Naela said. "There's a difference."

They fought in the glass-makers' foundry, among kilns that still hummed with furnace-songs. Corvhen was faster. Naela was patient.

stories.title: The Reef-Tender's Requiem
stories.text: Captain Merren Blackwater had drowned eight times. Each time, she returned—pulled back by the covenant that bound her to the Sapphire Deep's boundary-waters. A Deepkin captain sworn to keep the seals, a woman split across seven autonomous versions of herself, each one claiming the right to make the final sacrifice. The Tear of the Drowned God was dying. It needed a willing death—but which Merren would die? All of them? One? The Ghost-Harbormaster of a sunken port north of Greyport offered a third way: not a death, but a transformation. A sinking into the deep waters so thorough that the woman would become the covenant itself—no longer Merren, but the boundary, conscious and eternal. Merren's reflections convened in the boundary-pool where water flowed against gravity. Seven faces, identical save for small scars and differences of expression, looked at each other. "I don't want to disappear," Echo-Merren said. "None of us do," Mirror-Merren agreed. But the tide was rising. The boundary-waters were failing. Merfolk were beginning to breach. Surface-dwellers screamed at the coasts.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blindness
stories.text:
Henryk had drawn eight maps of eight dead worlds. The ninth map would trigger the tenth Ascension—or so the Wanderer had whispered into his ear in dreams. To prevent catastrophe, the young cartographer did what no mapmaker would dare: he burned out his own eyes. In the waystation north of Pelimor, where the Counter-Archive sheltered refugees and scholars, his blindness became a refuge. If Henryk couldn't see the world, he couldn't map it. If he couldn't map it, he couldn't complete the prophecy. But a refugee girl arrived—young, fever-bright, with fragments of pre-Remaking memory still humming in her bones. The bone-singers had marked her as important. The Wanderer's attention followed her like a shadow. "Draw my map," she asked Henryk. "Please. My mother is in a different version of the Burned Expanse. If I could see which one, I could reach her."

Henryk shook his head. "I won't. Maps are prophecy. Prophecy is a trap."

But the girl took his hand and placed it on her forehead.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Mercy
stories.text: In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where furnace-heat warped the very stones, Mella the apprentice glassblower discovered something the Dominion had overlooked: consciousness-fragments could be sealed inside molten glass without shattering. Every other smith in the quarter had failed. Every ledger-auditor sent to inspect her workshop had fled, whispering about singing vials that wept memory. She was not rebel by choice. Three weeks prior, the Dominion's tax-collectors had seized her master's forge, claiming the furnace-songs violated ledger-magic protocols. Mella watched them pack the bone-carved tools—tools that had belonged to her grandmother—into sealed crates. She watched her master kneel, accepting the "voluntary retraining" that meant a work-camp in the coastal cliffs. Now she worked alone in a hidden kiln beneath the Quarter's oldest district, where pre-Remaking stone still held memories of hotter days. The vials she crafted were perfect: each one held a fragment of someone's scattered self, sealed in amber glass, singing softly when moonlight touched them. They placed coins on her threshold—not gold anymore, but seeds, old cloth, promises.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Crimson Choice
stories.text:
Sorrath found the refugee camp in shadow—three hundred souls huddled beneath the Ironspine's eastern face, their children thin as winter grass. Gold was dust. The Dominion's auditors had moved on to other wastelands. And Sorrath, who had hoarded consciousness-fragments for decades, felt the weight of them differently now.

Inside a canvas shelter, an old woman clutched a child too weak to walk. The fragment Sorrath carried—a shard of a merchant's memory, sweet with commerce and belonging—sang to her pain. She needed it more than Sorrath needed to keep it.

Three centuries of dragons do not break easy. But the Remaking had broken everything else. When Sorrath placed the fragment in the woman's palm, its light steadied her breathing. The child's eyes opened.

No transaction. No ledger. Just mercy wearing scales.

stories.title: The Thief's Dark Bargain
stories.text: Korvin the Copper-Handed had stolen from every merchant-house in Saltmere except one: the Obsidian Archive, where the Dominion kept the dreaming ledgers—books that rewrote themselves with prophecy-ink. She had refused that job for five years. On the night of the Remaking's four-week anniversary, she changed her mind. Her sister was dying of shadow-sickness. The healers said consciousness-fragments could cure it, but only if they were *stolen* fragments—willingly given fragments lost their potency, became inert like ash. Korvin needed a theft impossible enough to catch the attention of the shadow-brokers who dealt in such currency. The Archive's vault lay seven floors beneath Keshtir's administrative heart, protected by locks that sang in frequencies that turned bone to marrow. Korvin's copper fingers could manipulate metal like flesh, but these locks required something else: a key made of memory and malice. She hired a bone-carver named Thael, promising him a consciousness-fragment of his own—her own fragment, stolen from herself through forbidden alchemy. The ritual cost her three years of life and left her unable to remember her sister's face clearly.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Last Map
stories.text: Tessa had been apprentice to three cartographers—all of them had gone blind, mad, or simply vanished into the Bleached Wastes. She inherited their task anyway: mapping the ninth iteration of the world, the one that the Remaking had revealed in fragmented glimpses. The prophecy was clear: whoever completed the map would trigger the Tenth Ascension. So she drew only partial maps. Incomplete charts. Deliberately wrong directions that led somewhere true by accident. Then she met Aldrin, who had survived the pre-human ruins and returned changed—his eyes reflecting stars that no longer existed. He carried fragments of the eighth world's cartography, drawn in a language that predated ink. Together, they climbed into the Ashen Reach, not to prevent the Tenth Ascension, but to understand what the ninth world actually *was*. In a canyon where water flowed upward, they found a city carved into the cliff-face, inhabited only by statues that moved when they weren't watched. The statues showed them maps drawn on their own skin.

stories.title: The Tide-Keeper's Vigil
stories.text: Captain Merenna of the Deepkin Guard had split into seven autonomous versions during the Remaking—each one believed she alone was real, and each one refused to die so the boundary could be sealed. The traditional covenant required a willing sacrifice. Seven versions meant seven wills, and they could not agree on who should surrender. But one of them, Echo-Merenna, proposed something the traditionalists called heresy: what if the boundary didn't need a death? What if it needed an *exchange*—something living offered freely, accepted freely, renewed freely? She dove into the Tear of the Drowned God alone, carrying a consciousness-fragment taken from a volunteer refugee—someone who had chosen, in writing, to give a piece of themselves to seal what had been broken. The fragment sang as it descended, a human voice calling to the dying god in the deep. The Drowned God, ancient and fractured itself, understood. The exchange was not a theft or a sacrifice. It was an acknowledgment: both human and god had been shattered by the Ascension. Both could offer what remained.
